bread_flour.name: Bread Flour (White)
bread_flour.functional_role: Provides primary structural framework via a strong gluten network.

substitution.effect: Increases water absorption significantly. Expect a denser crumb profile, shorter gluten strands, and a nuttier flavor. Increase hydration by 5-10% to compensate.

all_purpose_flour.name: All-Purpose Flour (Unbleached)
all_purpose_flour.functional_role: Provides moderate structure suitable for tender baked goods like cakes, cookies, and quick breads.

substitution.effect: Increases chewiness and structure. Cookies may become thicker and bread-like; cakes will become tough. Hydration may need a slight 2-3% increase.

rye_flour.name: Dark Rye Flour
rye_flour.functional_role: Adds dense texture, high enzyme activity (amylase), and complex earthy flavors. Contains high pentosans which compete for water.

substitution.effect: Wheat will introduce much more gluten-forming potential than rye. The dough will feel less sticky, be easier to handle, and rise higher.

cake_flour.name: Cake Flour (Bleached)
cake_flour.functional_role: Provides minimal gluten structure. Bleaching (chlorination) alters the starches to absorb more liquid and fat, yielding a highly tender, fine-crumbed texture.

substitution.ratio: Replace 2 tbsp of AP flour with cornstarch per cup
substitution.effect: Lowers the overall protein content to mimic cake flour, but lacks the chlorination, meaning it won't hold sugar and liquid quite as effectively in high-ratio cakes.

pastry_flour.name: Pastry Flour
pastry_flour.functional_role: Strikes a balance between cake and AP flour. Low enough protein to maintain flakiness in pie crusts and biscuits, but enough structure to prevent crumbling.

substitution.effect: Blends the mid-level protein of AP with the low-protein of cake flour to approximate the 9% protein target. Hydration needs remain mostly unchanged.

vital_wheat_gluten.name: Vital Wheat Gluten
vital_wheat_gluten.functional_role: Pure extracted gluten protein. Acts as a dough strengthener for low-protein flours (like rye or whole wheat), increasing volume, chewiness, and gas retention.

substitution.effect: If VWG is unavailable, replacing all AP or weak flour with high-protein bread flour is the only natural way to increase dough strength, though it will not be as dramatic.

sourdough_starter.name: Sourdough Starter (100% Hydration)
sourdough_starter.functional_role: Biological leavening (CO2 production) and flavor development (lactic/acetic acid).

substitution.effect: Replaces wild fermentation with commercial yeast. You will lose the signature lactic/acetic acid 'tang'. Dough will rise significantly faster.

sourdough_discard.name: Sourdough Discard (Unfed)
sourdough_discard.functional_role: Flavor enhancement, moisture retention, and tenderization due to degraded gluten.

substitution.effect: Discard is highly acidic with weak, broken-down gluten. If substituting out, you lose the distinct sourdough flavor notes and natural dough conditioning.

active_dry_yeast.name: Active Dry Yeast
active_dry_yeast.functional_role: Commercial biological leavening. Requires hydration in warm water (35-40°C) to break dormancy.

substitution.ratio: Use 0.75g Instant for every 1g Active Dry
substitution.effect: Instant yeast can be mixed directly into dry ingredients without blooming. Fermentation will occur roughly 15-20% faster.

diastatic_malt_powder.name: Diastatic Malt Powder
diastatic_malt_powder.functional_role: Introduces active amylase enzymes that break down complex flour starches into simple sugars. Feeds yeast during long fermentations and drastically improves crust browning.

substitution.effect: Provides immediate sugar for yeast and browning, but lacks the active enzymes. The dough will not benefit from the continuous starch-to-sugar breakdown during proofing.

butter.name: Unsalted Butter
butter.functional_role: Tenderizes crumb by coating flour proteins, provides dairy flavor, and adds steam for flakiness in laminated doughs.

substitution.effect: Maintains required fat levels but alters texture because coconut oil lacks butter's 16-18% water content. Bakes will turn out slightly crunchier/greasier; lacks classic dairy aroma.

olive_oil.name: Extra Virgin Olive Oil
olive_oil.functional_role: 100% lipid fat source. Extends shelf life, tenderizes crumb, and imparts herbaceous flavors (common in focaccia).

substitution.effect: Neutralizes the flavor profile entirely. The physical structure and moisture retention will remain identical.

vegetable_shortening.name: Vegetable Shortening
vegetable_shortening.functional_role: 100% lipid fat with a high melting point. Excellent for cutting into pie crusts to create extreme flakiness, and prevents cookies from spreading in the oven.

substitution.effect: Butter contains 20% water/milk solids, so bakes will spread more, brown faster, and be slightly less tender, but will gain a significantly better flavor.

whole_milk.name: Whole Milk
whole_milk.functional_role: Hydrates dry ingredients, enriches dough via milk fats, and promotes Maillard browning via lactose sugars.

substitution.effect: Stripping dairy fat and milk sugars (lactose) means the crust will brown much slower and the overall crumb texturing will lose its rich, tender mouthfeel.

buttermilk.name: Cultured Buttermilk
buttermilk.functional_role: Hydrates dough while providing a highly acidic environment (pH ~4.5). Tenderizes gluten, breaks down phytic acid, and reacts violently with baking soda for instant lift.

substitution.ratio: 1 cup Milk + 1 tbsp Vinegar (let sit 5 mins)
substitution.effect: Lowers the pH to react with baking soda, but lacks the lactic acid flavor profile and the viscosity of true cultured buttermilk.

heavy_cream.name: Heavy Whipping Cream (36% Fat)
heavy_cream.functional_role: A high-fat dairy liquid that aggressively tenderizes the crumb, shortens gluten strands, and provides an exceptionally rich, velvety mouthfeel (essential for scones and brioche).

substitution.ratio: 3/4 cup Milk + 1/4 cup Melted Butter per cup
substitution.effect: Replicates the fat content accurately, but the fat is not homogenized into the liquid the way it is in cream, which can lead to a slightly greasier final product.

oat_milk.name: Oat Milk (Barista/Plain)
oat_milk.functional_role: Hydrates ingredients and adds high levels of beta-glucans which natively retain moisture, keeping crumbs soft.

substitution.effect: Dairy milk adds structural casein proteins that oat milk lacks, making the resulting crumb slightly more robust and resilient.

soy_milk.name: Soy Milk (Unsweetened)
soy_milk.functional_role: Hydrates ingredients and provides high-protein structural stability comparable to dairy.

substitution.effect: Produces nearly identical structural results due to the matched protein content. Dairy will slightly increase crust browning.

milk_powder_whole.name: Dry Milk Powder (Whole)
milk_powder_whole.functional_role: Dough enhancer. Introduces high levels of lactose and casein proteins for deep Maillard browning and crumb softening without adding excess free water to the dough.

substitution.ratio: 1 cup liquid milk per 1/4 cup powder
substitution.effect: You must subtract 1 cup of water from the recipe's hydration to balance the liquid milk, which can alter the way the flour initially hydrates and develops gluten.

granulated_sugar.name: Granulated Sugar (Sucrose)
granulated_sugar.functional_role: Provides sweetness, speeds up yeast fermentation, retains moisture (hygroscopic), and drives caramelization/Maillard browning.

substitution.effect: Honey adds liquid moisture and speeds up crust browning drastically due to fructose. Lower your oven temperature by roughly 15°C (25°F) to avoid premature burning.

brown_sugar.name: Light Brown Sugar
brown_sugar.functional_role: Sucrose coated in molasses. Highly hygroscopic (retains moisture), lowers dough pH slightly (reacts with baking soda), and yields a softer, chewier final product than white sugar.

substitution.effect: Perfect structural and flavor match. Rub the molasses into the sugar manually until homogenous.

honey.name: Raw Honey
honey.functional_role: Invert sugar source. Increases shelf life via extreme moisture retention and speeds up crust browning drastically.

substitution.ratio: Use 1.25x the weight in sugar, add 15ml liquid
substitution.effect: Dough will brown slower in the oven and lack the slightly acidic, floral notes of honey. Staling process will begin slightly faster.

molasses.name: Unsulfured Molasses
molasses.functional_role: Liquid invert sugar rich in minerals. Highly acidic (pH ~5.5), retains incredible moisture to extend shelf life, and provides robust, bitter-sweet flavor notes.

substitution.altName: Dark Corn Syrup or Maple Syrup
substitution.effect: Replicates the liquid and sugar invert properties, preventing crystallization, but completely lacks the depth of flavor, acidity, and dark coloration of molasses.

glucose_syrup.name: Glucose Syrup
glucose_syrup.functional_role: Interfering agent. It consists of single-molecule sugars that physically get in the way of sucrose molecules, preventing sugar crystallization in caramels, glazes, and chewy cookies.

substitution.effect: Corn syrup contains added vanilla and sometimes salt, but structurally behaves the same way to prevent crystallization.

eggs.name: Whole Eggs
eggs.functional_role: Provides structural coagulation (whites), emulsification of fats and water (yolk lecithin), and aeration when whipped.

substitution.effect: Mimics structural binding and whipping properties decently well, but completely strips out the emulsifying lecithin and fats found in egg yolks. Crumb will be lighter and drier.

soy_lecithin.name: Soy Lecithin Powder
soy_lecithin.functional_role: Pure amphiphilic emulsifier. Binds water and fat molecules at a microscopic level, creating ultra-smooth batters, increasing volume, and preventing staling by slowing starch retrogradation.

substitution.ratio: 1 large yolk per 1 tbsp lecithin powder
substitution.effect: Introduces natural water and fat from the egg yolk, altering hydration slightly. Egg lecithin is excellent but not as concentrated as pure commercial soy lecithin.

baking_soda.name: Baking Soda (Sodium Bicarbonate)
baking_soda.functional_role: Chemical leavener. Requires an acidic ingredient (buttermilk, yogurt, honey) to react and produce CO2.

substitution.effect: Baking powder contains its own acid. Substituting will yield lift, but may leave the recipe's original acidic ingredients un-neutralized, altering flavor.

baking_powder.name: Baking Powder (Double-Acting)
baking_powder.functional_role: Complete chemical leavener. Contains sodium bicarbonate and two acidic salts. Reacts first when hydrated, and a second time when heated above 60°C (140°F) for maximum lift.

substitution.ratio: 1/4 tsp Baking Soda + 1/2 tsp Cream of Tartar = 1 tsp Baking Powder
substitution.effect: Creates a single-acting baking powder. The batter must be baked immediately upon mixing, as it will only react to hydration and will not get a second lift from oven heat.

cream_of_tartar.name: Cream of Tartar (Potassium Bitartrate)
cream_of_tartar.functional_role: Dry acid byproduct of winemaking. Stabilizes whipped egg whites by denaturing proteins, prevents sugar crystallization in syrups, and activates baking soda.

substitution.ratio: 1/2 tsp per 1/4 tsp Cream of Tartar
substitution.effect: Introduces liquid into the recipe. Works well for stabilizing egg whites, but cannot be used in dry mixes (like homemade baking powder).

bakers_ammonia.name: Baker's Ammonia (Ammonium Carbonate)
bakers_ammonia.functional_role: Thermal chemical leavener. Decomposes entirely into ammonia gas, carbon dioxide, and steam at 60°C. Leaves absolutely no alkaline residue, creating exceptionally crisp, dry cookies.

substitution.effect: Will provide the lift, but leaves behind chemical salts that hold onto moisture. The baked good will not achieve the same ultra-crisp, snappy texture.

citric_acid.name: Citric Acid (Anhydrous)
citric_acid.functional_role: Dry pH modifier. Drops the pH of dough to tighten the gluten network and boost yeast activity without adding liquid volume. Adds a sharp, sour flavor.

substitution.ratio: 1 tbsp juice per 1/4 tsp citric acid
substitution.effect: Introduces significant liquid volume, meaning the hydration of the dough must be adjusted to prevent a sticky, unmanageable crumb.

ascorbic_acid.name: Ascorbic Acid (Vitamin C)
ascorbic_acid.functional_role: Oxidizing agent/dough conditioner. Micro-doses (0.1%) tighten the gluten network, trap gas more effectively, and force the dough to rise higher and hold its shape.

substitution.effect: Introduces natural ascorbic acid but also adds citric acid and excess liquid, which can slightly alter the flavor profile and hydration.

dutch_cocoa_powder.name: Dutch-Processed Cocoa Powder
dutch_cocoa_powder.functional_role: Cocoa solids treated with an alkaline solution to neutralize acidity (pH raised to ~7.0-8.0). Darker color, smoother flavor, and absorbs liquid aggressively like flour.

substitution.effect: Natural cocoa is acidic (pH ~5.0). If you swap natural for Dutch, you must add baking soda (about 1/8 tsp per 3 tbsp of cocoa) to neutralize the acid, or the bake will be tart and dense.

cornstarch.name: Cornstarch
cornstarch.functional_role: Pure starch extracted from the corn endosperm. Gelatinizes at ~70°C (158°F) to thicken liquids without adding gluten. Creates a 'short' (tender) crumb in shortbreads and cookies.

substitution.effect: Potato starch gelatinizes at a slightly lower temperature and creates a more delicate, translucent gel, but behaves nearly identically to cornstarch in dry cake and cookie batters.

tapioca_starch.name: Tapioca Starch / Flour
tapioca_starch.functional_role: High-amylopectin starch. Gelatinizes at low temperatures to create a highly elastic, bouncy, and chewy texture (essential in Brazilian cheese bread/pão de queijo).

substitution.effect: Cornstarch has more amylose, which forms a rigid, opaque gel when cooled. It will thicken the mixture but completely destroy the 'stretchy/chewy' characteristic of tapioca.

almond_flour.name: Blanched Almond Flour
almond_flour.functional_role: Lipid-rich bulk ingredient. Provides zero gluten structure. The extremely high fat content tenderizes the crumb by lubricating other structural proteins (like eggs in a macaron).

substitution.effect: Replacing a 50% fat nut flour with a 1% fat wheat flour fundamentally breaks the recipe chemistry. Fat/liquid ratios must be entirely recalculated.

xanthan_gum.name: Xanthan Gum
xanthan_gum.functional_role: Hydrocolloid. Forms a highly viscous, pseudo-plastic gel that mimics the structural trapping properties of gluten in gluten-free baking.

substitution.effect: Guar gum is better for cold foods and retains moisture well, but doesn't provide the exact same elastic stretch (extensibility) as xanthan in hot baked goods.

l_cysteine.name: L-Cysteine
l_cysteine.functional_role: Reducing agent / Dough relaxer. An amino acid that actively breaks the disulfide bonds in gluten. Used to stop pizza dough or laminated doughs from shrinking/snapping back when rolled.

substitution.ratio: Use 2% deactivated yeast by baker's math
substitution.effect: Deactivated (dead) yeast naturally contains glutathione, which acts as a milder reducing agent to relax the dough over a slightly longer period.

calcium_propionate.name: Calcium Propionate
calcium_propionate.functional_role: Commercial mold inhibitor. Interferes with the cellular metabolism of molds and bacteria (rope) without significantly lowering the pH enough to kill the baker's yeast.

substitution.effect: The natural lactic and acetic acids produced by sourdough fermentation natively lower the pH to create a natural, albeit less aggressive, anti-microbial environment.

salt.name: Kosher Salt / Sea Salt
salt.functional_role: Enhances flavor, tightens the gluten network (makes dough stronger), and regulates/slows yeast fermentation.

substitution.ratio: Use roughly 50-60% by volume of Kosher salt (or 1:1 by weight)
substitution.effect: Table salt crystals are much finer. Measuring by volume will result in drastically over-salted dough. Always substitute salt by weight.
